UserProfile.title: UserProfile
UserProfile.description: Represents a student or general user of the college helpdesk system. Stores personal and contact information.

id.description: Unique identifier for the UserProfile entity.

name.description: The full name of the user.

email.description: The user's email address, used for communication and login.

phone.description: The user's phone number. Expected to be 10 digits, starting with 6-9.

registeredAt.description: Timestamp when the user registered in the system.

AdminProfile.title: AdminProfile
AdminProfile.description: Represents an administrator or helpdesk staff member. Stores administrative contact information.

id.description: Unique identifier for the AdminProfile entity.

name.description: The full name of the administrator.

email.description: The administrator's email address, used for communication and login.

phone.description: The administrator's phone number. Expected to be 10 digits, starting with 6-9.

hiredAt.description: Timestamp when the administrator was added to the system.

Ticket.title: Ticket
Ticket.description: Represents a support ticket submitted by a user, including its status and resolution details.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Ticket entity.

userId.description: Reference to the UserProfile who created this ticket. (Relationship: UserProfile 1:N Ticket)

title.description: The short title or subject of the issue.

description.description: A detailed description of the issue or request.

status.description: The current status of the ticket (e.g., 'Open', 'Solved', 'Unsolved').

category.description: The AI-suggested or manually assigned category/department for the ticket (e.g., 'IT Support', 'Admissions').

createdAt.description: Timestamp when the ticket was initially created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp when the ticket was last updated (e.g., status change, resolution notes added).

assignedAdminId.description: Optional reference to the AdminProfile who is currently assigned to or resolved this ticket. (Relationship: AdminProfile 1:N Ticket)

adminResolutionNotes.description: Notes added by an administrator regarding the resolution or response to the ticket.

definition.description: Stores individual user profiles (students/general users). The document ID `{userId}` matches the Firebase Authentication UID. Access is restricted to the owner (`request.auth.uid == userId`).

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique ID of the user, corresponding to their Firebase Authentication UID.

definition.description: Stores individual administrator profiles (helpdesk staff). The document ID `{adminId}` matches the Firebase Authentication UID. The existence of a document here signifies an 'admin' role. Self-modification is allowed; read access can be granted to all admins.

params.name: adminId
params.description: The unique ID of the administrator, corresponding to their Firebase Authentication UID.

definition.description: Stores all support tickets. Includes denormalized 'userId' for owner-based authorization independence. Users can only access their own tickets; administrators can access all tickets. 'assignedAdminId' is also denormalized within the document.

params.name: ticketId
params.description: The unique ID of the support ticket.

firestore.reasoning: This Firestore structure is designed to adhere strictly to the core design principles, focusing on authorization independence, clarity, and debuggability. Authorization Independence: 1. Users (`/users/{userId}`): User profiles are stored in a top-level collection where the document ID (`{userId}`) directly corresponds to the Firebase Authentication UID. This inherently provides authorization independence as rules can simply compare `request.auth.uid` with the `{userId}` wildcard in the path, eliminating any need for `get()` calls to a parent document or separate role checks. A user can only access their own profile. 2. Admins (`/admins/{adminId}`): Similar to users, admin profiles are stored in a top-level collection. The existence of a document at `/admins/$(request.auth.uid)` serves as the primary mechanism for Database Access Control (DBAC). Rules can check for admin status using `exists(/databases/$(database)/documents/admins/$(request.auth.uid))` without relying on complex, nested authorizations. For updating their own profile, rules can compare `request.auth.uid` with `{adminId}`. 3. Tickets (`/tickets/{ticketId}`): Each ticket document explicitly includes the `userId` of the creator and an optional `assignedAdminId`. This denormalization means that rules for ticket access (read/write by the user who created it) can directly check `request.auth.uid == resource.data.userId`. For administrators, the denormalized `userId` is also present, allowing easy access while the admin role itself is checked via the `/admins` collection, again avoiding `get()` calls from a ticket to its user or admin for authorization decisions. Support for Query-Aware Permissions (QAPs): 1. Users (`/users/{userId}`): Secure `list` operations are supported by ensuring rules permit reads only when `request.auth.uid == userId`. Any `list` query must be filtered by the current user's UID to retrieve only their profile, or more typically, direct `get` requests for the user's own document are used. 2. Admins (`/admins/{adminId}`): For administrators, `list` operations can be securely permitted for other administrators. The mere existence of an admin's UID in this collection grants the 'admin' role, enabling broad read access to tickets or other admin functions, while ensuring only authenticated admins can access this list. 3. Tickets (`/tickets/{ticketId}`): This structure is optimized for QAPs for both users and administrators. User Access: To view their own tickets, users must issue queries with `where('userId', '==', request.auth.uid)`. The security rules can then enforce this filter, ensuring users only retrieve their own tickets and preventing unauthorized `list` access to other users' tickets. Admin Access: Administrators, by virtue of their presence in the `/admins` collection, can be granted permission to `list` all tickets without requiring a specific filter, as their role itself authorizes broad access. Clarity of Intent and Debuggability: The use of distinct top-level collections (`/users`, `/admins`, `/tickets`) for different entities with clearly defined ownership fields (`userId`, `adminId`) makes security rules simple to write and debug. Authorization logic is explicit and does not rely on complex hierarchical traversal. The `status` field in `Ticket` provides explicit state modeling, enhancing clarity. Wildcards are descriptive (`{userId}`, `{adminId}`, `{ticketId}`).
